articles.title: Erasing Existentials
articles.summary:
Erasing Existentials is a programming language feature that allows developers to use existential quantifiers in their code. The key technology here is the concept of "dyn Trait", which represents an existential type with a specific trait. This means that while Rust's type system guarantees that any value of type dyn Trait conforms to its underlying type, it does not provide information about what values of type dyn Trait might be. To address this, we can use the "for-exists conversion" technique, which allows us to express existential types in a way that is more explicit and easier to reason about.

The article explains how to use the for-exists conversion to write an existential type S: Trait<B> where B is another existentially quantified type variable. It also discusses the benefits of using these techniques in Rust's type system, such as making it easier to reason about code and improving performance by avoiding unnecessary type conversions.

articles.title: Saying Goodbye to Asm.js
articles.summary: Axe-time, sword-time, shields are sundered, Wind-time, wolf-time, ere the world falls. – Völuspá , Poetic Edda As of Firefox 148, SpiderMonkey's asm.js optimizations are disabled by default and we plan to remove the code entirely in a future release. If you maintain a site that uses asm.js, nothing will break. asm.js is just a subset of plain JavaScript, so the code keeps running through our regular JIT just like any other script. That said, recompiling to WebAssembly will get you faster execution and smaller binaries. History: asm.js was Mozilla’s response to the question posed by NaCl and PNaCl : how can the web run code at native speeds? The idea was clever: pick a strict, statically-typed subset of JavaScript that an engine could recognize on the fly and compile down to native code.

articles.title: GitHub Source Code Breach - TeamPCP Claims Access to Internal Source Code
articles.summary:
GitHub Source Code Breach: A Targeted Attack on Internal Systems

The article highlights a significant breach of GitHub's internal systems by the threat actor TeamPCP, which allegedly exfiltrated proprietary organization data and source code. The attackers are offering the stolen dataset for sale on underground cybercrime forums, demanding offers exceeding $50, 000. According to the threat actor's post, the compromised data encompasses approximately 4, 000 private repositories tied directly to GitHub's main platform.

GitHub has confirmed an ongoing investigation into the matter and is investigating unauthorized access to its internal repositories. The company acknowledges the unauthorized access but seeks to reassure users regarding the safety of customer data. While they currently have no evidence of impact to customer information stored outside of GitHub’s internal repositories, they are closely monitoring their infrastructure for follow-on activity.

articles.title: Why Ruby Still Feels Like Home After All These Years
articles.summary: Ruby, a language created by Yukihiro "Yuki" Matsumoto and Garth "Graul" Jones in 1995, has become one of the most popular programming languages for web development. Despite its age, it remains highly regarded due to its simplicity, readability, and versatility. Ruby's core technology is called the Ruby Core Language (RCL), which includes features such as dynamic typing, object-oriented programming, and a rich set of libraries and frameworks. The key reason why Ruby still feels like home after all these years is that it has been around for over 15 years, making it accessible to developers across various industries. Additionally, its popularity in the web development community, with projects like Rails, Ruby on Rails (RoR), and Ruby on Rails 3, shows its continued relevance and impact on modern software development practices.

articles.title: Autoregressive next token prediction and KV Cache in transformers
articles.summary:
In recent years, researchers have explored various approaches to improve the performance of Transformers, a type of neural network used for natural language processing tasks. One such approach is autoregressive next-token prediction (ANTP), which aims to predict the most likely next tokens in a sequence based on the previous tokens. ANT can be seen as a form of attention-based model that predicts the next token given the context of the current token.

KV Cache, also known as key-value cache, is another important technology used by Transformers for efficient data processing and storage. It stores key-value pairs (e.g., user information, document metadata) in memory to speed up subsequent operations on those values.

Both ANT and KV Cache are crucial components that contribute significantly to the performance of Transformers, enabling them to handle complex natural language tasks more effectively.
